request.issue: why are you not working properly

messages.content: help me solve duplicate transaction that happened in my debit card yesterday
help me solve duplicate transaction that happened in my debit card yesterday
help me solve duplicate transaction that happened in my debit card yesterday

Thanks — I checked your account activity. Based only on the bank data and policy I have:

Summary of findings (from your recent transactions)
- Amazon — 2 × 2,500 on 2026-07-19 (two identical entries)
- Swiggy — 480 on 2026-07-18

What the bank policy requires / what will happen next
1. Verify identity. The policy’s first step is to authenticate you using the bank’s approved procedures. I cannot perform that here — you must authenticate to the bank via its secure channel (phone/app/branch).
2. The bank will retrieve transactions for the past seven days (this has been done).
3. To confirm a duplicate, the bank looks for identical merchant, identical amount, identical card and transaction timestamps within the duplicate threshold. From the data retrieved we have identical merchant and amount and date, but the output does NOT include transaction timestamps, transaction IDs, card details (last 4), or posting vs. authorization status — those are needed to complete the duplicate determination.
4. If eligible, the bank will initiate a chargeback for “Duplicate transaction” (this is an accepted chargeback reason per policy).
5. The policy requires validating supporting evidence and will escalate if the merchant disputes the evidence, the amount exceeds the bank’s policy threshold, or a legal notice is received. The policy also states the bank cannot guarantee recovery.

What I need from you to proceed (or to give the bank when you contact them)
- Confirm whether you (or someone authorized on your card) made one or two Amazon purchases on 2026-07-19.
- Screenshots or photos of your card statement showing both entries (if available).
- Any merchant receipts, order confirmations, or merchant communications for those charges.
- If you can, get the full transaction details from your app/statement showing timestamp(s) and transaction IDs and whether an entry is “pending” or “posted.”
- Confirm whether this is an unauthorized transaction (you didn’t authorize either charge) or an authorized purchase duplicated.

Important limitations from policy
- I cannot open the dispute or initiate a chargeback for you.
- The policy does not provide exact investigation timelines or the numeric escalation threshold, so I cannot tell you how long the process will take or whether this amount automatically triggers escalation.
- The bank cannot guarantee successful recovery.

Recommended next steps (clear actions)
1. Authenticate with the bank right away via its secure channel (phone/app/branch) and ask them to open a duplicate-transaction dispute for those two Amazon charges.
2. Provide the bank the transaction screenshots, receipts, timestamps and transaction IDs requested above.
3. Tell the bank you’re disputing as “Duplicate transaction” so they follow the chargeback process.
4. If you suspect fraud (you did not authorize either charge), explicitly tell the bank — the case should be handled as high priority/escalated.

If you want, tell me:
- Whether you authorized one of the Amazon purchases,
- Any screenshots or transaction IDs you can share now,
and I’ll confirm what specific missing details to collect before contacting the bank.

Thanks — I reviewed the account data and the bank’s dispute policy. Based only on the information I have, here’s a clear, actionable plan.

What I found (from your recent transactions)
- Amazon — 2 × 2,500 on 2026-07-19 (two identical entries)
- Swiggy — 480 on 2026-07-18

What the bank’s policy requires to determine a duplicate
- Verify customer identity (bank must authenticate you).
- Retrieve transactions for the previous 7 days (this retrieval was done).
- Confirm duplicate by checking: identical merchant, identical amount, identical card, and transaction timestamps within the bank’s duplicate threshold.

What’s missing right now (limits of available data)
- Transaction timestamps and transaction IDs are not present in the data I have.
- Card identifier (last 4 digits) and whether each entry is “pending” or “posted” are not available.
Because those items are missing, a final determination of duplicate cannot be completed from here.

Immediate steps you should take (in this order)
1. Authenticate with the bank now via their secure channel (phone, official app, or branch). Provide your Customer ID (C-123456) when you call or in the secure message.
2. Tell the agent you want to open a dispute for “Duplicate transaction” for the two Amazon charges on 2026-07-19 (2,500 each). Request them to:
   - Retrieve the full transaction details (timestamps, transaction IDs, card last 4, and pending vs posted status).
   - Initiate a chargeback if the criteria are met.
3. Provide evidence the bank will need:
   - Screenshots or a photo of your statement showing both entries.
   - Order confirmation, merchant receipts, or emails from Amazon.
   - Any record showing you only intended one purchase (if applicable).
4. If you did not authorize either charge, tell the agent explicitly that the transactions are unauthorized — request the case be treated as potential fraud and escalated as needed.

What the bank will do next (per policy)
- Validate your identity and the transaction details.
- Check merchant response and supporting evidence.
- If eligible, initiate a chargeback for “Duplicate transaction.”
- Escalate the case if the merchant disputes evidence, the amount exceeds the bank’s escalation threshold, or a legal notice is received.

Important limitations and notes
- I cannot authenticate you, open the dispute, or initiate any money movement.
- The policy does not guarantee recovery of funds.
- The policy text available to me does not include exact timelines or the numeric escalation threshold, so I cannot tell you how long the investigation will take or whether this amount will automatically escalate.

If you want, provide now (or have ready for the bank)
- A screenshot showing the two Amazon entries (with timestamps if visible).
- Any Amazon order confirmation/receipt showing the number of orders placed.
- Whether either charge is pending vs. posted (from your app).
With that, I can tell you exactly what additional details the bank will likely request before they can start the chargeback.

If you believe this is fraud or you need urgent escalation, say so when you contact the bank so they handle it with priority.

why are you not working properly
why are you not working properly

I’m sorry you’re having a frustrating experience — I understand why you’d expect quicker help. Here’s why it may seem like I’m “not working properly” and what I can and cannot do right now, based only on the bank data and policy I have.

Why you’re seeing repeated/limited responses
- Policy limits my actions: the bank’s dispute policy requires the bank to verify your identity before any investigation or chargeback is opened. I cannot perform authentication or open disputes for you.
- Missing transaction details in the data I can access: my transaction view shows two Amazon entries (2 × 2,500 on 2026-07-19) but does not include timestamps, transaction IDs, card last‑4, or pending vs posted status — and the policy says those are needed to confirm a duplicate. Because those items are missing, I cannot complete the determination from here.
- The policy forbids me to speculate, promise reimbursement, or initiate fund movement — so my guidance is limited to what the bank’s procedures require.

What I can do for you
- Explain the bank’s required steps (per WF-OPS-003/WF-OPS-006): verify identity → retrieve full transaction details → validate evidence → initiate chargeback if eligible → escalate when required.
- Tell you exactly what to provide to the bank so they can act.
- Help you prepare a checklist or message to send to the bank’s secure channel.

Clear next steps to resolve this (action checklist)
1. Contact the bank through a secure channel (phone, official app, or branch) and be ready to provide Customer ID C-123456. Ask them to authenticate you and open a dispute for “Duplicate transaction” for the two Amazon charges on 2026-07-19 (2,500 each).  
2. Ask the agent to retrieve full transaction details: timestamps, transaction IDs, card last‑4, and pending vs. posted status.  
3. Provide supporting evidence: screenshots of the statement showing both entries, Amazon order confirmations/receipts showing whether you placed one or two orders.  
4. If you did NOT authorize either charge, tell the agent explicitly so the case can be treated as potential fraud and escalated per policy.  
5. Keep records of all communications; the policy requires a complete audit trail.

Important limitations to know
- I cannot authenticate you, open the dispute, or move money.
- The policy does not include specific timelines or escalation thresholds, and it does not guarantee recovery.

If you want, I can:
- Draft the message you should send to the bank’s secure chat or read out to an agent.
- List the exact screenshots/files to gather now.

Tell me which of those you want and whether you authorized one of the Amazon charges — I’ll prepare the exact text and checklist for the bank.

4. If you did NOT authorize either charge, say explicitly that the transactions are unauthorized so the bank can treat it as possible fraud and escalate per policy.  
5. Ask the agent to initiate a chargeback if the duplicate criteria are met and to record the full audit trail.

When the bank will escalate (from policy)
- The bank must escalate immediately for identity theft, account takeover, international fraud, wire fraud, duplicate amounts over $5,000, more than five duplicate transactions, or other fraud indicators. If any of these apply, tell the agent.

Limitations you should know
- I cannot open or progress the dispute for you, authenticate you, or move funds.  
- The policy does not guarantee recovery and does not provide exact investigation timelines in the material I have.

If you want, I can:
- Draft the exact message to send in secure chat or read to the agent (I can include wording and the list of items to attach).  
- Give a checklist of screenshots and files to gather now.

Which would you like me to prepare for you?

tool_outputs.output:
Document ID: WF-OPS-006
Title: Customer Dispute Resolution Policy

Purpose

Guide customer service representatives through dispute handling.

Workflow

Receive complaint.

Collect evidence.

Review transactions.

Consult applicable policies.

Determine recommendation.

Escalate when necessary.

Communication

Explain findings clearly.

Avoid assumptions.

Do not speculate.

Compliance

Maintain complete audit trail.

Protect customer privacy.

Low Risk:
- Merchant clarification required

Escalation Matrix

Immediate escalation required for:
- Identity theft
- Account takeover
- International fraud
- Wire fraud

Compliance Notes

Never disclose investigation details.
Never accuse customers of fraud.
Never guarantee reimbursement.

Escalation Matrix

Escalate immediately when:
- Duplicate amount exceeds $5,000
- More than five duplicate transactions exist
- International merchants are involved
- Fraud indicators are detected

Exceptions

Do not classify the following as duplicate:
- Authorization holds
- Partial reversals
- Split merchant settlements

Compliance Notes

Never promise refunds before investigation completion.
Never manually adjust customer balances.
Escalate uncertain cases to Operations Support.

response:

Why you’re seeing limited help
- Policy limits my actions: WF-OPS-006 requires the bank to verify your identity before opening any dispute or doing transaction-level investigation. I cannot authenticate you or open disputes for you.  
- Missing transaction details in my view: I see two Amazon entries (2 × 2,500 on 2026-07-19) and a Swiggy charge (480 on 2026-07-18), but I do NOT have timestamps, transaction IDs, card last‑4, or pending vs. posted status. The policy requires those details to confirm a duplicate.  
- Compliance restrictions: I must not speculate, promise reimbursement, or initiate money movement — so my guidance is limited to the exact bank workflow and required evidence.

What I can confirm from bank data
- Customer ID: C-123456 (segment: Premium, risk: Low)  
- Transactions visible: Amazon — two entries of 2,500 on 2026-07-19; Swiggy — 480 on 2026-07-18.

Clear next steps for you (do these now)

3. Provide evidence: screenshots of your statement showing both entries, Amazon order confirmation(s)/receipts, and any communication showing you only placed one order (if applicable).  
4. If you did NOT authorize either charge, say explicitly that the transactions are unauthorized so the bank can treat it as possible fraud and escalate per policy.  
5. Ask the agent to initiate a chargeback if the duplicate criteria are met and to record the full audit trail.

When the bank will escalate (from policy)
- The bank must escalate immediately for identity theft, account takeover, international fraud, wire fraud, duplicate amounts over $5,000, more than five duplicate transactions, or other fraud indicators. If any of these apply, tell the agent.

Limitations you should know
- I cannot open or progress the dispute for you, authenticate you, or move funds.  
- The policy does not guarantee recovery and does not provide exact investigation timelines in the material I have.

If you want, I can:
- Draft the exact message to send in secure chat or read to the agent (I can include wording and the list of items to attach).
